source: National Institute of Standards and Technology

functions.name: GOVERN
functions.description: The organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, expectations, and policy are established, communicated, and monitored. The GOVERN Function provides outcomes to inform what an organization may do to achieve and prioritize the outcomes of the other five Functions in the context of its mission and stakeholder expectations.

categories.name: Organizational Context
categories.description: The circumstances — mission, stakeholder expectations, dependencies, and legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements — surrounding the organization's cybersecurity risk management decisions are understood.

subcategories.description: The organizational mission is understood and informs cybersecurity risk management
implementation_examples: Establish and communicate the organization's mission statement and how cybersecurity supports it
Document the relationship between critical business objectives and cybersecurity risk tolerance
Review and update mission-cybersecurity alignment annually or when the mission changes
informative_references: NIST SP 800-39: Managing Information Security Risk

subcategories.description: Internal and external stakeholders are understood, and their needs and expectations regarding cybersecurity risk management are understood and considered
implementation_examples: Identify key internal stakeholders (board, executives, IT, legal) and external stakeholders (regulators, partners, customers)
Document stakeholder cybersecurity expectations through interviews, surveys, or contractual review
Maintain a stakeholder register that maps expectations to cybersecurity activities

subcategories.description: Legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements regarding cybersecurity — including privacy and civil liberties obligations — are understood and managed
implementation_examples: Maintain a compliance obligations register covering all applicable laws, regulations, and contracts
Map regulatory requirements (HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS, state breach notification laws) to specific cybersecurity controls
Conduct periodic legal and regulatory landscape reviews to identify new or changing obligations
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PM-6 (Measures of Performance)

subcategories.description: Critical objectives, capabilities, and services that external stakeholders depend on or expect from the organization are understood and communicated
implementation_examples: Identify and document business-critical services and their dependencies on cybersecurity
Communicate service level expectations and cybersecurity assurance levels to external partners
Conduct business impact analysis to prioritize critical capabilities
informative_references: NIST SP 800-34 Rev. 1: Contingency Planning Guide

subcategories.description: Outcomes, capabilities, and services that the organization depends on are understood and communicated
implementation_examples: Map external dependencies including supply chain, cloud services, and third-party integrations
Document single points of failure and develop contingency plans for critical dependencies
Establish communication protocols with key dependency providers for cybersecurity incidents
informative_references: NIST SP 800-161 Rev. 1: Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management

categories.name: Risk Management Strategy
categories.description: The organization's priorities, constraints, risk tolerance and appetite statements, and assumptions are established, communicated, and used to support operational risk decisions.

subcategories.description: Risk management objectives are established and agreed to by organizational stakeholders
implementation_examples: Define organizational risk appetite and tolerance levels with executive leadership sign-off
Document risk management objectives that align with business strategy and mission
Communicate approved risk management objectives to all relevant personnel

subcategories.description: Risk appetite and risk tolerance statements are established, communicated, and maintained
implementation_examples: Define quantitative and qualitative risk appetite statements for different risk categories
Establish risk tolerance thresholds that trigger escalation or additional controls
Review and update risk appetite statements annually or following significant incidents
informative_references: NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1: Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments

subcategories.description: Cybersecurity risk management activities and outcomes are included in enterprise risk management processes
implementation_examples: Integrate cybersecurity risk registers into the enterprise risk management framework
Include cybersecurity risk metrics in enterprise risk dashboards and board reports
Ensure cybersecurity risk assessments follow the same methodology as other enterprise risk domains

subcategories.description: Strategic direction that describes appropriate risk response options is established and communicated
implementation_examples: Define and document risk response strategies: accept, avoid, mitigate, transfer, share
Provide guidelines for selecting appropriate risk response options based on risk level
Communicate risk response decision authority levels across the organization

subcategories.description: Lines of communication across the organization are established for cybersecurity risks, including risks from suppliers and other third parties
implementation_examples: Establish formal reporting channels for cybersecurity risk escalation from operational to executive levels
Create cross-functional cybersecurity risk committees with defined meeting cadences
Implement supply chain risk communication protocols with third-party vendors

categories.name: Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities
categories.description: Cybersecurity roles, responsibilities, and authorities to foster accountability, performance assessment, and continuous improvement are established and communicated.

subcategories.description: Organizational leadership is responsible and accountable for cybersecurity risk and fosters a culture that is risk-aware, ethical, and continually improving
implementation_examples: Assign a senior executive (CISO or equivalent) with explicit accountability for cybersecurity risk
Include cybersecurity risk as a standing agenda item in board and executive committee meetings
Establish tone-from-the-top communications emphasizing cybersecurity culture and accountability
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PM-1 (Information Security Program Plan)

subcategories.description: Roles, responsibilities, and authorities related to cybersecurity risk management are established, communicated, understood, and enforced
implementation_examples: Define a RACI matrix for cybersecurity responsibilities across all departments
Document cybersecurity roles and responsibilities in job descriptions and organizational policies
Conduct periodic reviews to ensure roles remain aligned with organizational structure changes
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PM-2 (Information Security Program Leadership Role)

subcategories.description: Adequate resources are allocated commensurate with the cybersecurity risk strategy, roles, responsibilities, and policies
implementation_examples: Develop and maintain a cybersecurity budget aligned with risk assessment outcomes
Conduct workforce planning to ensure adequate cybersecurity staffing levels
Allocate dedicated resources for cybersecurity training, tools, and incident response capabilities
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PM-3 (Information Security and Privacy Resources)

subcategories.description: Cybersecurity is included in human resources practices
implementation_examples: Incorporate cybersecurity responsibilities into employee onboarding and offboarding procedures
Include cybersecurity performance metrics in employee evaluations for relevant roles
Conduct background checks for personnel with access to sensitive systems and data
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PS family (Personnel Security)

categories.name: Policy
categories.description: Organizational cybersecurity policy is established, communicated, and enforced.

subcategories.description: Policy for managing cybersecurity risks is established based on organizational context, cybersecurity strategy, and priorities and is communicated and enforced
implementation_examples: Develop a comprehensive cybersecurity policy framework approved by senior leadership
Ensure policies are accessible to all employees and reviewed at least annually
Implement policy acknowledgment tracking to verify employee awareness
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PL-1 (Policy and Procedures)

subcategories.description: Policy for managing cybersecurity risks is reviewed, updated, communicated, and enforced to reflect changes in requirements, threats, technology, and organizational mission
implementation_examples: Establish a policy review cycle (at minimum annually) with defined triggers for off-cycle reviews
Track policy exceptions and waivers with expiration dates and risk acceptance documentation
Use version control and change management processes for policy updates
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PL-1 (Policy and Procedures)

subcategories.description: Cybersecurity policy is used to define and guide implementation of cybersecurity procedures throughout the organization
implementation_examples: Map organizational policies to specific operational procedures and standards
Develop implementation guides that translate policy requirements into actionable steps
Conduct compliance audits to verify procedures align with policy directives
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PL-2 (System Security Plans)

categories.name: Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management
categories.description: Cyber supply chain risk management processes are identified, established, managed, monitored, and improved by organizational stakeholders.

subcategories.description: A cybersecurity supply chain risk management program, strategy, objectives, policies, and processes are established and agreed to by organizational stakeholders
implementation_examples: Develop a formal C-SCRM program with documented strategy, policies, and procedures
Define supply chain risk appetite and tolerance specific to cybersecurity concerns
Obtain executive sponsorship and resource allocation for the C-SCRM program

subcategories.description: Cybersecurity roles and responsibilities for suppliers, customers, and partners are established, communicated, and coordinated internally and externally
implementation_examples: Include cybersecurity requirements and responsibilities in all supplier contracts and SLAs
Define shared responsibility models for cloud services and third-party integrations
Establish joint cybersecurity communication channels with critical suppliers

subcategories.description: Cybersecurity supply chain risk management is integrated into cybersecurity and enterprise risk management, risk assessment, and improvement processes
implementation_examples: Include supply chain risks in enterprise risk registers and risk assessment processes
Conduct periodic supply chain risk assessments for critical vendors and service providers
Incorporate supply chain risk findings into continuous improvement activities

subcategories.description: Suppliers are known and prioritized by criticality
implementation_examples: Maintain a comprehensive inventory of all suppliers with access to organizational systems or data
Categorize suppliers by criticality level based on data access, system integration, and business impact
Conduct tiered due diligence assessments based on supplier criticality classification

subcategories.description: Requirements to address cybersecurity risks in supply chains are established, prioritized, and integrated into contracts and other agreements with suppliers and other relevant third parties
implementation_examples: Develop standard cybersecurity contract clauses for different supplier tiers
Require suppliers to demonstrate compliance with relevant security standards (SOC 2, ISO 27001)
Include right-to-audit clauses and incident notification requirements in supplier agreements

functions.name: IDENTIFY
functions.description: The organization's current cybersecurity risks are understood. Understanding its assets (e.g., data, hardware, software, systems, facilities, services, people), suppliers, and related cybersecurity risks enables an organization to prioritize its efforts consistent with its risk management strategy and the mission needs identified under GOVERN.

categories.name: Asset Management
categories.description: Assets (e.g., data, hardware, software, systems, facilities, services, people) that enable the organization to achieve business purposes are identified and managed consistent with their relative importance to organizational objectives and the organization's risk strategy.

subcategories.description: Inventories of hardware managed by the organization are maintained
implementation_examples: Deploy automated asset discovery tools to maintain real-time hardware inventory
Implement network access control (NAC) to identify and track connected devices
Conduct periodic physical audits to reconcile hardware inventory with discovery results
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CM-8 (System Component Inventory)
CIS Controls v8: Control 1 (Inventory and Control of Enterprise Assets)

subcategories.description: Inventories of software, services, and systems managed by the organization are maintained
implementation_examples: Maintain a software asset inventory including version numbers, license status, and deployment locations
Use software composition analysis tools to track all software components and dependencies
Catalog cloud services and SaaS applications using a cloud access security broker (CASB)
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CM-8 (System Component Inventory)
CIS Controls v8: Control 2 (Inventory and Control of Software Assets)

subcategories.description: Representations of the organization's authorized network communication and internal and external network data flows are maintained
implementation_examples: Create and maintain network architecture diagrams showing all authorized communication paths
Document internal and external data flows including data classification levels
Use network flow monitoring tools to validate actual traffic against authorized data flow documentation
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AC-4 (Information Flow Enforcement)

subcategories.description: Inventories of services provided by suppliers are maintained
implementation_examples: Maintain a catalog of all third-party services including vendor contacts, contract details, and data handling
Track service level agreements and cybersecurity requirements for each supplier service
Regularly reconcile supplier service inventory with procurement and accounts payable records

subcategories.description: Assets are prioritized based on classification, criticality, resources, and impact to the mission
implementation_examples: Implement a data classification scheme (public, internal, confidential, restricted) applied to all assets
Conduct business impact analysis to assign criticality ratings to systems and services
Use asset classification to drive security control selection and resource allocation decisions

subcategories.description: Inventories of data and corresponding metadata for designated data types are maintained
implementation_examples: Implement data discovery and classification tools to identify and catalog sensitive data
Maintain a data inventory with metadata including owner, classification, retention period, and storage location
Create and maintain data flow maps that track data throughout its lifecycle

subcategories.description: Systems, hardware, software, services, and data are managed throughout their life cycles
implementation_examples: Implement lifecycle management processes covering acquisition, deployment, maintenance, and decommissioning
Track end-of-life and end-of-support dates for all hardware and software assets
Develop and execute secure disposal procedures for assets reaching end of life
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: SA-22 (Unsupported System Components)

categories.name: Risk Assessment
categories.description: The cybersecurity risk to the organization, assets, and individuals is understood by the organization.

subcategories.description: Vulnerabilities in assets are identified, validated, and recorded
implementation_examples: Conduct regular vulnerability scans across all network-accessible assets using automated tools
Perform application security testing including SAST, DAST, and SCA for critical applications
Maintain a vulnerability database with severity ratings, affected assets, and remediation status
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: RA-5 (Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning)
CIS Controls v8: Control 7 (Continuous Vulnerability Management)

subcategories.description: Cyber threat intelligence is received from information sharing forums and sources
implementation_examples: Subscribe to threat intelligence feeds relevant to the organization's industry and threat landscape
Participate in information sharing organizations (ISACs/ISAOs) for sector-specific threat intelligence
Integrate threat intelligence into security monitoring and risk assessment processes
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PM-16 (Threat Awareness Program)
NIST SP 800-150: Guide to Cyber Threat Information Sharing

subcategories.description: Internal and external threats to the organization are identified and recorded
implementation_examples: Maintain a threat register documenting known internal and external threat actors and vectors
Conduct periodic threat modeling exercises for critical systems and applications
Use frameworks like STRIDE, MITRE ATT&CK, or PASTA to systematically identify threats
informative_references: NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1: Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments

subcategories.description: Potential impacts and likelihoods of threats exploiting vulnerabilities are identified and recorded
implementation_examples: Use risk matrices to assess likelihood and impact of identified threat-vulnerability pairs
Conduct quantitative risk analysis using methods like FAIR for high-priority risks
Document risk scenarios with potential business impact estimates for executive communication

subcategories.description: Threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts are used to understand inherent risk and inform risk response prioritization
implementation_examples: Aggregate risk assessment data to produce an organizational risk profile and heat map
Prioritize risk treatment based on residual risk levels and organizational risk appetite
Present risk assessment results and treatment recommendations to leadership for decision-making

subcategories.description: Risk responses are chosen, prioritized, planned, tracked, and communicated
implementation_examples: Develop risk treatment plans with specific actions, owners, timelines, and success criteria
Track risk treatment plan progress through regular reviews and status reporting
Communicate risk response decisions and residual risk acceptance to relevant stakeholders
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PM-4 (Plan of Action and Milestones)

categories.name: Improvement
categories.description: Improvements to the organization's cybersecurity risk management processes, procedures, and activities are identified across all CSF Functions.

subcategories.description: Improvements are identified from evaluations
implementation_examples: Conduct periodic self-assessments and gap analyses against the NIST CSF and relevant standards
Perform internal audits of cybersecurity controls and processes to identify improvement opportunities
Engage external assessors for independent evaluations of cybersecurity posture

subcategories.description: Improvements are identified from security tests and exercises, including those done in coordination with suppliers and relevant third parties
implementation_examples: Conduct regular penetration testing and red team exercises to identify security weaknesses
Perform tabletop exercises and incident response simulations to test preparedness
Include third-party and supply chain scenarios in security testing programs
informative_references: NIST SP 800-84: Guide to Test, Training, and Exercise Programs

subcategories.description: Improvements are identified from execution of operational processes, procedures, and activities
implementation_examples: Implement after-action reviews following security incidents, changes, and exercises
Track and analyze cybersecurity operational metrics to identify trends and improvement needs
Establish a lessons-learned repository accessible to all cybersecurity personnel

subcategories.description: Incident response plans and other cybersecurity plans that affect operations are established, communicated, maintained, and improved
implementation_examples: Develop and maintain a comprehensive incident response plan with defined roles and procedures
Conduct regular reviews and updates of cybersecurity plans based on lessons learned
Ensure all relevant personnel are trained on and have access to current cybersecurity plans
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: IR-1 (Incident Response Policy and Procedures)
NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2: Computer Security Incident Handling Guide

functions.name: PROTECT
functions.description: Safeguards to manage the organization's cybersecurity risks are used. Once assets and risks are identified and prioritized, PROTECT supports the ability to secure those assets to prevent or lower the likelihood and impact of adverse cybersecurity events.

categories.name: Identity Management, Authentication, and Access Control
categories.description: Access to physical and logical assets is limited to authorized users, services, and hardware and managed commensurate with the assessed risk of unauthorized access.

subcategories.description: Identities and credentials for authorized users, services, and hardware are managed by the organization
implementation_examples: Implement centralized identity management with automated provisioning and deprovisioning workflows
Enforce unique identity assignment for all users, service accounts, and hardware devices
Conduct periodic access reviews and recertification for all identity types
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: IA family (Identification and Authentication)
CIS Controls v8: Control 6 (Access Control Management)

subcategories.description: Identities are proofed and bound to credentials based on the context of interactions
implementation_examples: Implement identity proofing procedures aligned with NIST SP 800-63A assurance levels
Bind credentials to verified identities through secure enrollment processes
Use risk-based authentication that adjusts assurance requirements based on transaction context

subcategories.description: Users, services, and hardware are authenticated
implementation_examples: Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all privileged and remote access
Deploy certificate-based authentication for services and hardware devices
Enforce strong password policies and phishing-resistant authentication methods
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: IA-2 (Identification and Authentication)
NIST SP 800-63B: Authentication and Lifecycle Management

subcategories.description: Identity assertions are protected, conveyed, and verified
implementation_examples: Use secure federation protocols (SAML, OIDC) for identity assertion exchange
Implement token-based authentication with appropriate expiration and refresh mechanisms
Validate identity assertions at each relying party before granting access
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: IA-8 (Identification and Authentication - Non-Organizational Users)

subcategories.description: Access permissions, entitlements, and authorizations are defined in a policy, managed, enforced, and reviewed, and incorporate the principles of least privilege and separation of duties
implementation_examples: Implement role-based access control (RBAC) with documented authorization policies
Enforce least privilege through regular access reviews and automated privilege reduction
Implement separation of duties controls for critical business and security functions
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AC-2 (Account Management), AC-6 (Least Privilege)

subcategories.description: Physical access to assets is managed, monitored, and enforced commensurate with risk
implementation_examples: Implement physical access control systems with badge readers and visitor management
Monitor physical access through CCTV and security personnel at sensitive areas
Conduct periodic reviews of physical access permissions and facility security assessments
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PE family (Physical and Environmental Protection)

categories.name: Awareness and Training
categories.description: The organization's personnel are provided with cybersecurity awareness and training so that they can perform their cybersecurity-related tasks.

subcategories.description: Personnel are provided with awareness and training so that they possess the knowledge and skills to perform general tasks with cybersecurity risks in mind
implementation_examples: Deliver annual cybersecurity awareness training to all employees covering phishing, social engineering, and safe computing
Conduct periodic phishing simulations and provide targeted training based on results
Maintain training records and track completion rates across the organization
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AT-2 (Literacy Training and Awareness)
NIST SP 800-50: Building an Information Technology Security Awareness and Training Program

subcategories.description: Individuals in specialized roles are provided with awareness and training so that they possess the knowledge and skills to perform relevant tasks with cybersecurity risks in mind
implementation_examples: Provide role-specific security training for developers, administrators, and incident responders
Support professional certifications and continuing education for cybersecurity staff
Conduct hands-on training exercises and simulations relevant to specialized roles
informative_references: NICE Workforce Framework for Cybersecurity (SP 800-181 Rev. 1)

subcategories.description: Senior executives understand their roles and responsibilities for cybersecurity and how to fulfill them
implementation_examples: Provide targeted cybersecurity briefings for board members and executives quarterly
Conduct executive tabletop exercises focused on cyber incident decision-making
Deliver training on regulatory obligations and fiduciary duties related to cybersecurity
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AT-3(5) (Processing Personally Identifiable Information)
SEC Cybersecurity Risk Management, Strategy, Governance Rules (2023)

categories.name: Data Security
categories.description: Data are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information.

subcategories.description: The confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-at-rest are protected
implementation_examples: Encrypt sensitive data at rest using AES-256 or equivalent encryption standards
Implement file integrity monitoring (FIM) for critical data stores and configuration files
Deploy data loss prevention (DLP) controls for data repositories containing sensitive information
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)
NIST SP 800-111: Guide to Storage Encryption Technologies

subcategories.description: The confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-in-transit are protected
implementation_examples: Enforce TLS 1.2 or higher for all data in transit across networks
Implement VPN or encrypted tunnels for sensitive communications over untrusted networks
Deploy certificate management and monitoring to ensure encryption is properly maintained
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity)
NIST SP 800-52 Rev. 2: Guidelines for TLS Implementations

subcategories.description: The confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-in-use are protected
implementation_examples: Implement endpoint DLP controls to monitor and protect data while being processed
Use memory encryption and secure enclaves for processing sensitive data where available
Apply application-level controls to prevent unauthorized data access during processing
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: SC-4 (Information in Shared System Resources)

subcategories.description: Backups of data are created, protected, maintained, and tested
implementation_examples: Implement automated backup schedules with appropriate retention policies for all critical data
Store backups in geographically separate locations with encryption and access controls
Conduct regular backup restoration tests to verify data integrity and recovery capability

categories.name: Platform Security
categories.description: The hardware, software (e.g., firmware, operating systems, applications), and services of physical and virtual platforms are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect their confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

subcategories.description: Configuration management practices are established and applied
implementation_examples: Establish and enforce secure configuration baselines for all systems using CIS Benchmarks or DISA STIGs
Implement automated configuration management and drift detection tools
Maintain configuration change management processes with approval workflows and audit trails
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CM-2 (Baseline Configuration), CM-6 (Configuration Settings)
CIS Controls v8: Control 4 (Secure Configuration of Enterprise Assets and Software)

subcategories.description: Software is maintained, replaced, and removed commensurate with risk
implementation_examples: Implement automated patch management processes with defined SLAs based on vulnerability severity
Maintain a software lifecycle management program tracking end-of-life and unsupported software
Remove unauthorized software through application whitelisting and periodic audits
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: SI-2 (Flaw Remediation), CM-11 (User-Installed Software)

subcategories.description: Hardware is maintained, replaced, and removed commensurate with risk
implementation_examples: Track hardware warranty, end-of-life, and end-of-support dates with automated alerts
Implement secure hardware disposal procedures including data sanitization and destruction certification
Maintain spare hardware inventory for critical systems to ensure rapid replacement
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: MA-2 (Controlled Maintenance), MA-6 (Timely Maintenance)
NIST SP 800-88 Rev. 1: Guidelines for Media Sanitization

subcategories.description: Log records are generated and made available for continuous monitoring
implementation_examples: Configure comprehensive logging across all systems, applications, and network devices
Centralize log collection in a SIEM or log management platform with defined retention periods
Protect log integrity through write-once storage, encryption, and access controls
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU family (Audit and Accountability)
NIST SP 800-92: Guide to Computer Security Log Management

subcategories.description: Installation and execution of unauthorized software is prevented
implementation_examples: Deploy application whitelisting or software restriction policies on endpoint systems
Implement code signing requirements for internally developed applications
Use endpoint detection tools to block execution of unauthorized or malicious software
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CM-7 (Least Functionality), CM-11 (User-Installed Software)

categories.name: Technology Infrastructure Resilience
categories.description: Security architectures are managed with the organization's risk strategy to protect asset confidentiality, integrity, and availability, and organizational resilience.

subcategories.description: Networks and environments are protected from unauthorized logical access and usage
implementation_examples: Implement network segmentation using firewalls, VLANs, and micro-segmentation
Deploy zero trust architecture principles for network access decisions
Monitor and enforce network access policies using intrusion prevention systems
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: SC-7 (Boundary Protection), AC-4 (Information Flow Enforcement)

subcategories.description: The organization's technology assets are protected from environmental threats
implementation_examples: Implement environmental controls including fire suppression, HVAC, and water detection in data centers
Deploy uninterruptible power supplies (UPS) and backup generators for critical infrastructure
Conduct periodic environmental risk assessments for facilities housing critical technology
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PE-9 through PE-18

subcategories.description: Mechanisms are implemented to achieve resilience requirements in normal and adverse situations
implementation_examples: Deploy redundant systems and failover mechanisms for critical services and infrastructure
Implement load balancing and auto-scaling to maintain availability during demand spikes
Design disaster recovery architecture with defined RPO and RTO for all critical systems
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CP-2 (Contingency Plan), SI-13 (Predictable Failure Prevention)

subcategories.description: Adequate resource capacity to ensure availability is maintained
implementation_examples: Implement capacity monitoring with alerting thresholds for compute, storage, and network resources
Conduct capacity planning exercises aligned with business growth projections
Use auto-scaling and elastic cloud resources to dynamically adjust to demand

functions.name: DETECT
functions.description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed. DETECT enables timely discovery and analysis of anomalies, indicators of compromise, and other potentially adverse cybersecurity events that may indicate that cybersecurity attacks and incidents are occurring.

categories.name: Continuous Monitoring
categories.description: Assets are monitored to find anomalies, indicators of compromise, and other potentially adverse events.

subcategories.description: Networks and network services are monitored to find potentially adverse events
implementation_examples: Deploy network intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS) at network boundaries and critical segments
Implement network traffic analysis tools to identify anomalous patterns and behaviors
Monitor DNS, DHCP, and proxy logs for indicators of compromise and data exfiltration
informative_references: CIS Controls v8: Control 13 (Network Monitoring and Defense)

subcategories.description: The physical environment is monitored to find potentially adverse events
implementation_examples: Deploy security cameras, motion sensors, and alarm systems in sensitive areas
Implement physical intrusion detection systems with real-time alerting
Monitor environmental sensors (temperature, humidity, water) in data center facilities
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PE-6 (Monitoring Physical Access), PE-20

subcategories.description: Personnel activity and technology usage are monitored to find potentially adverse events
implementation_examples: Implement user behavior analytics (UBA) to detect insider threat indicators
Monitor privileged account activity with enhanced logging and session recording
Deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to monitor system activity
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AC-2(12) (Account Monitoring for Atypical Usage)
CIS Controls v8: Control 8 (Audit Log Management)

subcategories.description: External service provider activities and services are monitored to find potentially adverse events
implementation_examples: Monitor cloud service provider security dashboards and compliance status
Implement CASB or cloud-native security tools to monitor third-party service activity
Review external provider security reports (SOC 2, penetration tests) on a regular cadence
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: SA-9 (External System Services)

subcategories.description: Computing hardware and software, runtime environments, and their data are monitored to find potentially adverse events
implementation_examples: Deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) across all managed endpoints
Implement application runtime monitoring and anomaly detection
Monitor container and serverless runtime environments for suspicious activities
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: SI-4 (System Monitoring), SI-7 (Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity)

categories.name: Adverse Event Analysis
categories.description: Anomalies, indicators of compromise, and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents.

subcategories.description: The baseline of expected behavior for networks and expected data flows for users and systems is established and maintained
implementation_examples: Document and maintain baselines for normal network traffic patterns and data flows
Implement machine learning-based behavioral analytics to establish and update baselines
Regularly review and update baselines to account for legitimate changes in the environment
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: SI-4(13) (Analyze Traffic/Event Patterns)

subcategories.description: Potentially adverse events are analyzed to better understand associated activities
implementation_examples: Correlate events from multiple data sources using SIEM to identify attack patterns
Perform root cause analysis on detected anomalies to determine if they indicate actual incidents
Use threat intelligence to enrich event analysis and identify known attack techniques
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: IR-4 (Incident Handling), SI-4 (System Monitoring)

subcategories.description: Information is correlated from multiple sources
implementation_examples: Aggregate and normalize security event data from all sources into a centralized SIEM
Create correlation rules that combine indicators across network, endpoint, and application layers
Implement automated threat hunting workflows that cross-reference multiple data sources
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: AU-6(5) (Integrated Analysis of Audit Records)

subcategories.description: The estimated impact and scope of adverse events are understood
implementation_examples: Develop impact assessment procedures that evaluate the scope and severity of detected events
Use asset criticality ratings to prioritize event investigation and response
Implement automated triage workflows that estimate event impact based on affected assets

subcategories.description: Information on adverse events is provided to authorized staff and tools
implementation_examples: Configure automated alerting and notification workflows for detected adverse events
Integrate detection outputs with incident management and ticketing systems
Provide real-time security dashboards accessible to SOC analysts and incident responders

subcategories.description: Cyber threat intelligence and other contextual information are integrated into the analysis
implementation_examples: Integrate threat intelligence feeds with SIEM for automated indicator enrichment
Correlate detected events with known threat actor tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs)
Use threat intelligence platforms to provide context during event analysis and investigation
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PM-16 (Threat Awareness Program)

functions.name: RESPOND
functions.description: Actions regarding a detected cybersecurity incident are taken. RESPOND supports the ability to contain the impact of cybersecurity incidents. Outcomes within this Function cover incident management, analysis, mitigation, reporting, and communication.

categories.name: Incident Management
categories.description: Responses to detected cybersecurity incidents are managed.

subcategories.description: The incident response plan is executed in coordination with relevant third parties once an incident is declared
implementation_examples: Activate the incident response plan with defined command structure and communication protocols
Engage pre-identified third parties (legal counsel, forensics, PR, law enforcement) as appropriate
Document all incident response actions with timestamps for post-incident review

subcategories.description: Incident reports are triaged and validated
implementation_examples: Implement standardized incident triage procedures with defined severity classification criteria
Validate reported incidents through initial investigation and evidence collection
Prioritize incident response based on validated severity, scope, and business impact

subcategories.description: Incidents are categorized and prioritized
implementation_examples: Apply incident categorization taxonomy (data breach, malware, DDoS, insider threat, etc.)
Assign priority levels based on organizational impact, data sensitivity, and regulatory requirements
Adjust categorization and priority as new information emerges during investigation

subcategories.description: Incidents are escalated or elevated as needed
implementation_examples: Define escalation criteria and paths for different incident severity levels
Implement automated escalation triggers based on time thresholds and incident characteristics
Ensure executive notification procedures are followed for high-severity incidents

subcategories.description: The criteria for initiating incident recovery are applied
implementation_examples: Define clear criteria for transitioning from incident response to recovery operations
Document containment verification requirements before beginning system restoration
Obtain approval from incident commander and system owners before initiating recovery
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CP-10 (System Recovery and Reconstitution)

categories.name: Incident Analysis
categories.description: Investigations are conducted to ensure effective response and support forensics and recovery activities.

subcategories.description: Analysis is performed to establish what has taken place during an incident and the root cause of the incident
implementation_examples: Conduct timeline analysis to reconstruct the sequence of events during the incident
Perform root cause analysis using forensic evidence, logs, and system artifacts
Document attack vectors, exploited vulnerabilities, and threat actor techniques used
informative_references: NIST SP 800-86: Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques

subcategories.description: Actions performed during an investigation are recorded, and the records' integrity and provenance are preserved
implementation_examples: Maintain a chain of custody log for all digital evidence and forensic artifacts
Use write-protected storage and cryptographic hashing to preserve evidence integrity
Document all investigative actions with timestamps, personnel, and methodologies used

subcategories.description: Incident data and metadata are collected, and their integrity and provenance are preserved
implementation_examples: Implement automated log and artifact collection during incident response
Preserve original evidence using forensic imaging and write-blocking techniques
Store collected data in secure, access-controlled repositories with integrity verification
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: IR-4 (Incident Handling), AU-9 (Protection of Audit Information)

subcategories.description: An incident's magnitude is estimated and validated
implementation_examples: Assess the number of affected systems, users, and data records during an incident
Evaluate business impact including financial losses, operational disruption, and reputational damage
Update magnitude estimates as additional information becomes available during investigation

categories.name: Incident Response Reporting and Communication
categories.description: Response activities are coordinated with internal and external stakeholders as required by laws, regulations, or policies.

subcategories.description: Internal and external stakeholders are notified of incidents
implementation_examples: Follow defined notification procedures for internal teams, management, and board as appropriate
Comply with regulatory breach notification requirements (state laws, HIPAA, GDPR) within mandated timeframes
Coordinate with law enforcement notification as required by policy or legal obligation

subcategories.description: Information is shared with designated internal and external stakeholders
implementation_examples: Share indicators of compromise with ISACs, ISAOs, and trusted partners through established channels
Provide regular incident status updates to executive leadership and affected business units
Coordinate with peer organizations and government agencies to share actionable threat intelligence
informative_references: NIST SP 800-150: Guide to Cyber Threat Information Sharing
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: PM-15 (Security and Privacy Groups and Associations)

subcategories.description: Coordination with affected parties is conducted for effective response and recovery
implementation_examples: Establish coordination procedures with incident response partners, vendors, and service providers
Conduct joint response activities with affected third parties and supply chain partners
Maintain current contact information and communication channels for all coordination parties

categories.name: Incident Mitigation
categories.description: Activities are performed to prevent expansion of an event and to mitigate its effects.

subcategories.description: Incidents are contained
implementation_examples: Execute containment strategies appropriate to the incident type (network isolation, account disabling, system quarantine)
Implement short-term containment to stop immediate damage while preserving evidence
Deploy long-term containment measures that allow business operations to continue during remediation

subcategories.description: Incidents are eradicated
implementation_examples: Remove malware, unauthorized access mechanisms, and attacker persistence mechanisms from affected systems
Patch or mitigate the vulnerabilities that were exploited during the incident
Verify eradication completeness through scanning, monitoring, and forensic analysis
informative_references: NIST SP 800-83 Rev. 1: Guide to Malware Incident Prevention and Handling

subcategories.description: Newly identified vulnerabilities are mitigated or documented as accepted risks
implementation_examples: Prioritize remediation of vulnerabilities discovered during incident investigation
Apply emergency patches or compensating controls for exploited vulnerabilities
Document risk acceptance decisions for vulnerabilities that cannot be immediately remediated
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: RA-5 (Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning)
NIST SP 800-40 Rev. 4: Guide to Enterprise Patch Management Planning

functions.name: RECOVER
functions.description: Assets and operations affected by a cybersecurity incident are restored. RECOVER supports timely restoration of normal operations to reduce the impact from cybersecurity incidents and enable appropriate communication during recovery efforts.

categories.name: Incident Recovery Plan Execution
categories.description: Restoration activities are performed to ensure operational availability of systems and services affected by cybersecurity incidents.

subcategories.description: The recovery portion of the incident response plan is executed once initiated from the incident response process
implementation_examples: Execute system restoration procedures following verified containment and eradication
Restore systems from known-good backups or rebuild from trusted baselines
Verify system integrity and functionality before returning systems to production
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CP-10 (System Recovery and Reconstitution)

subcategories.description: Recovery actions are selected, scoped, prioritized, and performed
implementation_examples: Prioritize recovery of critical systems based on business impact analysis and organizational priorities
Execute recovery in phases, validating each phase before proceeding to the next
Track recovery progress against defined RPO and RTO objectives
informative_references: NIST SP 800-34 Rev. 1: Contingency Planning Guide Section 4

subcategories.description: The integrity of backups and other restoration assets is verified before using them for restoration
implementation_examples: Verify backup integrity through checksum validation and test restoration before using in recovery
Scan backup media for malware and indicators of compromise before restoration
Maintain offline or immutable backup copies to ensure availability of clean restoration points
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CP-9 (System Backup), CP-10

subcategories.description: Critical mission functions and cybersecurity risk management are considered to establish post-incident operational norms
implementation_examples: Establish enhanced monitoring and validation procedures for recovered systems
Update security controls and monitoring based on lessons learned from the incident
Define criteria for returning to normal operational status from heightened security posture

subcategories.description: The integrity of restored assets is verified, systems and services are restored, and normal operating status is confirmed
implementation_examples: Perform comprehensive system validation and security testing on restored assets
Conduct user acceptance testing to verify service functionality post-recovery
Obtain formal sign-off from system owners and security team before declaring recovery complete
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CP-10 (System Recovery and Reconstitution)

categories.name: Incident Recovery Communication
categories.description: Restoration activities and progress are communicated to designated internal and external stakeholders.

subcategories.description: Recovery activities and progress in restoring operational capabilities are communicated to designated internal and external stakeholders
implementation_examples: Provide regular recovery status updates to executive leadership and affected business units
Communicate recovery timelines and milestones to customers and partners affected by the incident
Issue formal communications when services are fully restored and normal operations resume
informative_references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: CP-2(1) (Coordinate with Related Plans)

subcategories.description: Public updates on incident recovery are shared using approved methods and messaging
implementation_examples: Coordinate public communications with legal counsel, PR team, and executive leadership
Provide accurate and timely updates through approved channels (website, email, press releases)
Maintain consistent messaging across all communication channels during recovery

subcategories.description: Stakeholders are informed of lessons learned and risk mitigation improvements resulting from incident recovery
implementation_examples: Conduct formal post-incident reviews and share findings with relevant stakeholders
Document lessons learned and incorporate them into updated policies, procedures, and training
Communicate planned security improvements and their implementation timelines to stakeholders

tiers.name: Partial
tiers.description: Application of the organizational cybersecurity risk strategy is managed in an ad hoc manner. Prioritization is ad hoc and not formally based on objectives or threat environment. Cybersecurity risk management is performed irregularly, with limited awareness of cybersecurity risks at the organizational level. The organization implements cybersecurity risk management on an irregular, case-by-case basis. The organization may not have processes to enable cybersecurity information sharing.

tiers.name: Risk Informed
tiers.description: Risk management practices are approved by management but may not be established as organization-wide policy. Prioritization of cybersecurity activities and protection needs is directly informed by organizational risk objectives, the threat environment, or business requirements. There is awareness of cybersecurity risks at the organizational level, but an organization-wide approach to managing cybersecurity risks has not been established. The organization knows its role in the larger ecosystem with respect to dependencies and dependents but has not formalized its capabilities for interacting and sharing information externally.

tiers.name: Repeatable
tiers.description: The organization's risk management practices are formally approved and expressed as policy. Organizational cybersecurity practices are regularly updated based on the application of risk management processes to changes in business requirements, threat and technology landscape. An organization-wide approach to manage cybersecurity risks has been established using risk-informed policies, processes, and procedures, defined and implemented as intended, and reviewed. Consistent methods are in place to respond effectively to changes in risk. Personnel possess the knowledge and skills to perform their roles and responsibilities. The organization consistently and accurately monitors cybersecurity risk of organizational assets. The organization shares cybersecurity information with partners, suppliers, and other third parties.

tiers.name: Adaptive
tiers.description: There is an organization-wide approach to managing cybersecurity risks that uses risk-informed policies, processes, and procedures to address potential cybersecurity events. The relationship between cybersecurity risks and organizational objectives is clearly understood and considered when making decisions. Senior executives monitor cybersecurity risks in the same context as financial risk and other organizational risks. The organization adapts its cybersecurity practices based on previous and current cybersecurity activities, including lessons learned and predictive indicators. Through a process of continuous improvement incorporating advanced cybersecurity technologies and practices, the organization actively adapts to a changing cybersecurity landscape and responds in a timely and effective manner to evolving, sophisticated threats.
